content.text: Azure Availability Zones give you two fundamentally different ways to achieve zone resilience. Zonal (zone-pinned) services put the responsibility for spreading resources on you; zone-redundant services put it on the platform. Both protect against a single zone failure, but they operate at different layers and suit different types of workloads.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison: Zonal resources pinned to specific zones vs Zone-Redundant services spread by the platform

content.text: When you deploy a zonal resource, you explicitly choose which Availability Zone it lives in by passing --zone 1, --zone 2, or --zone 3 (or the equivalent in IaC). The resource exists in exactly that physical datacenter. A zone failure takes that resource offline; resources in other zones continue running.

The key implication is that zone resilience requires application-level design. If you want a zone-tolerant VM deployment, you must create separate VMs in each zone yourself, set up a load balancer, and define a failover mechanism. The platform does not move or replicate zonal resources automatically.
the resource lives in one zone permanently. It does not migrate during a zone failure.
related zonal resources (VM + managed disk + NIC) must all be in the same zone to avoid cross-zone charges and latency.
to achieve three-zone coverage you deploy three separate resource instances, one per zone.
traffic that leaves one zone and enters another incurs a $0.01/GB charge; zonal architecture makes this flow visible and controllable.

# Deploy a VM pinned to Zone 1
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-zone1 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --zone 1 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Deploy a companion VM in Zone 2 for redundancy
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-zone2 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --zone 2 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# VM Scale Set spread across all 3 zones (zonal spread, not zone-redundant)
az vmss create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --vm-sku Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --instance-count 6 \
  --zones 1 2 3

# Verify zone placement
az vm show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-zone1 \
  --query zones \
  --output tsv

Zone-redundant services require no zone selection. You provision the resource once and the platform internally distributes its instances, replicas, or shards across all Availability Zones in the region. During a zone failure, the remaining zone copies continue serving traffic without operator intervention.

Zone redundancy is typically enabled by selecting a higher SKU tier or by toggling a flag. It cannot be retrofitted onto the Basic or Standard tiers of most services — you must choose a Premium or zone-aware SKU at creation time.
you never specify --zone; the platform makes the placement decision.
for storage and databases, data is written to all zone copies before the write is acknowledged, ensuring RPO near zero.
all zone copies serve live traffic in normal operation; zone resilience comes with built-in load distribution.
zone-redundant configurations carry 99.99% or higher SLAs versus 99.9% for single-zone equivalents.

# Zone-redundant Storage (ZRS): no zone flag, just set SKU
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageacct \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_ZRS

# Zone-redundant Azure SQL (Business Critical)
az sql db create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --edition BusinessCritical \
  --family Gen5 \
  --capacity 8 \
  --zone-redundant true

# Zone-redundant Cosmos DB
az cosmosdb update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mycosmosaccount \
  --locations regionName=eastus failoverPriority=0 isZoneRedundant=true

# Zone-redundant Service Bus (Premium tier)
az servicebus namespace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySBNamespace \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Premium \
  --zone-redundant true
No zone flag; flag omitted or set to zone-redundant SKU
Transparent; remaining zones absorb load automatically
Synchronous (storage, DB) or automatic multi-zone distribution
Near-zero (synchronous replication means no data loss)
Depends on LB health probe + failover time
VMs, VMSS, managed disks, Ultra Disks, NVAs
Storage (ZRS), SQL, Cosmos DB, LB, App GW v2, Key Vault
No visible charge to customer (absorbed by service)
Stateful compute needing zone awareness and affinity
Stateless managed services, databases, ingress layer
Combining Both in a Single Architecture

Production-grade architectures use zone-redundant services for managed layers and zonal (zone-spread) resources for compute. The load balancer and database tiers use zone-redundant services; the application tier uses zone-spread VMs or pods so the platform and the application each handle their respective resilience.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Build a zone-resilient three-tier stack combining both models

RG="myRG"
LOCATION="eastus"

# ---- TIER 1: Zone-redundant ingress (Standard LB with zone-redundant Public IP) ----
az network public-ip create -g $RG -n myPIP --sku Standard --allocation-method Static
az network lb create -g $RG -n myLB --sku Standard --public-ip-address myPIP \
  --frontend-ip-name myFE --backend-pool-name myBE

# ---- TIER 2: Zonal compute (VMSS spread across zones 1, 2, 3) ----
az vmss create -g $RG -n myVMSS --image Ubuntu2204 --vm-sku Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --instance-count 3 --zones 1 2 3 --lb myLB --backend-pool-name myBE

# ---- TIER 3: Zone-redundant data layer ----
# Storage
az storage account create -g $RG -n mystorageacct -l $LOCATION --sku Standard_ZRS

# SQL
az sql db create -g $RG --server mysqlserver -n mydb \
  --edition BusinessCritical --family Gen5 --capacity 8 --zone-redundant true
A common mistake is enabling zone redundancy on the database and load balancer but deploying compute in a single zone. The weakest link determines zone resilience — always check every layer.
Decision Guide: Which Model to Use
Use the following criteria to decide whether to use zonal or zone-redundant deployment for each component in your architecture:
Is this a managed service (storage, database, messaging, load balancer)? Use zone-redundant if the service supports it — the SKU cost is usually justified by the operational simplicity.
Is this compute (VMs, VMSS, NVAs, bare metal)? Use zonal placement with explicit zone spreading — zone-redundant is not available for IaaS compute.
Does the resource require zone affinity (VM + attached managed disk must be in the same zone)? Use zonal and ensure all co-located resources share the same zone.
Is the workload stateless (web frontends, microservices)? Prefer zone-redundant or VMSS zone-spread with autoscaling — the load balancer distributes across zones automatically.
Does the service lack a zone-redundant SKU? Fall back to manual zone-spread with separate instances per zone and an application-level load balancer.
Are you constrained to a lower-cost SKU? Accept the zone limitation and plan for manual DR procedures in the event of a zone outage.
IaC: Expressing Both Models in Bicep
Mixing zonal and non-zonal resources without a load balancer:
if a VM in Zone 1 is the only route to a database, zone-redundancy on the database provides no benefit because the compute layer is a single point of failure.
Assuming Standard LB is always zone-redundant:
the Standard Load Balancer frontend IP is zone-redundant only when you omit the zones property on the Public IP. Assigning it to a specific zone (zones: ['1']) pins it to that zone.

a VMSS with --zones 1 2 3 spreads VMs across zones, but it is not zone-redundant in the managed-service sense. If Zone 1 fails, the VMs in Zone 1 go offline; the VMSS autoscaler must add replacements.

a managed disk must be in the same zone as its attached VM. Creating a disk without a zone and attaching it to a zonal VM causes an implicit zone assignment, but detaching and re-attaching across zones fails.
Using Basic SKU expecting zone support:
Basic Load Balancer and Basic Public IP have no Availability Zone support at all.
Zone Affinity: Why Zonal Resources Must Stay Together

Zonal resources carry zone affinity requirements. A VM and its managed OS disk must be in the same zone. A NIC must be in the same zone as the VM it is attached to. Violating zone affinity is either blocked by the API or causes hidden cross-zone latency and cost.

This zone affinity requirement is the main reason zonal design is more complex than zone-redundant: each logical component (VM + disk + NIC) becomes a zone-specific unit, and you must replicate that unit across zones manually to achieve resilience.

# Create a zone-pinned managed disk (must match the VM's zone)
az disk create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDataDisk-zone1 \
  --size-gb 128 \
  --sku Premium_LRS \
  --zone 1         # Must match the VM's zone

# Attach to a Zone 1 VM
az vm disk attach \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vm-name myVM-zone1 \
  --name myDataDisk-zone1

# Attempting to attach a Zone 2 disk to a Zone 1 VM would fail:
# "The disk myDataDisk-zone2 does not support the zone of the VM myVM-zone1"

# Ultra Disk: zone selection is mandatory (Ultra Disks are always zonal)
az disk create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myUltraDisk \
  --size-gb 512 \
  --sku UltraSSD_LRS \
  --zone 2 \
  --disk-iops-read-write 10000 \
  --disk-mbps-read-write 500

ZRS (Zone-Redundant Storage) is the most commonly used zone-redundant service and illustrates how platform-managed replication works. Understanding it concretely helps demystify what zone-redundant means in practice.

When you write a blob to a ZRS storage account, the write is sent to three separate storage clusters — one per zone — simultaneously. The API call returns 201 Created only after all three clusters acknowledge the write. If Zone 1 fails after that point, the data is available from Zones 2 and 3 with no failover needed.
DR with regional failover, no zone protection
DR with read access to secondary without failover

# Show replication type and failover capability of a storage account
az storage account show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageacct \
  --query "{replication:sku.name, failoverStatus:failoverInProgress}" \
  --output json

# Convert ZRS to GZRS for added regional DR (live migration, no downtime)
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageacct \
  --sku Standard_GZRS

# List all storage accounts in a resource group with their replication tiers
az storage account list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[*].{name:name, replication:sku.name}" \
  --output table
Impact of Zone Failures: Contrasting Behavior
The behavior of your application during a zone failure depends entirely on which deployment model you used. The following scenarios illustrate the difference:
Scenario A: Zonal VMs Without Zone Spreading

You deploy a single VM in Zone 1 and attach a managed disk in Zone 1. Zone 1 experiences a power outage. Result: the VM is offline. The disk is inaccessible. Your application is completely down until Zone 1 recovers or you manually create a replacement in Zone 2 or 3. Recovery time depends on how quickly you detect the failure and provision a new VM — typically 5 to 30 minutes for a practiced team.
Scenario B: Zonal VMs Spread Across Zones

You deploy three VMs, one per zone, behind a Standard Load Balancer with zone-redundant frontend IP. Zone 1 experiences a power outage. Result: the LB health probe detects that Zone 1's VM is unhealthy within 10 to 30 seconds. Traffic is redirected to Zones 2 and 3. Your application continues serving at two-thirds capacity, and autoscaling can bring up a replacement in Zone 2 or 3 within 3 to 5 minutes. Data on the Zone 1 disk is inaccessible until the zone recovers.

You use Azure SQL with zone_redundant=true. Zone 1 experiences a power outage. Result: the SQL primary replica was in Zone 1. Within seconds, the platform promotes a HA secondary from Zone 2 or Zone 3 to primary. Your application's connection string does not change; the Azure SQL listener redirects connections automatically. New connections may see 10 to 30 seconds of intermittent errors during the promotion window, but the data is fully intact (RPO = 0, because writes were quorum-acknowledged across zones).

Scenario C is the target state for managed layers. The operational difference between B and C is who detects and reacts to the failure: the load balancer + autoscaler in B, versus the platform's internal HA manager in C. Platform-managed is faster and requires zero runbook.

Zone-redundant services typically cost more per unit because the platform pays for three-zone replication internally. Zonal services can be cheaper per unit but require more resource instances to achieve the same zone coverage.
Absorbed by service, not billed to customer
SQL Business Critical vs General Purpose
Zone redundancy requires Premium (10x Standard price)

For compute, zone-spreading three VMs does not cost more per VM, but you pay for three VMs instead of one. Zone-redundant managed databases, however, can significantly increase costs because they require higher tiers. Always evaluate the zone resilience cost against the cost of downtime before choosing a tier.

# Zonal VM in Zone 1 (explicit zone selection)
resource "azurerm_linux_virtual_machine" "zone1" {
  name                = "myVM-zone1"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  size                = "Standard_DS2_v2"
  zone                = "1"   # Zone-pinned

  admin_username = "azureuser"
  admin_ssh_key {
    username   = "azureuser"
    public_key = file("~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub")
  }

  network_interface_ids = [azurerm_network_interface.zone1.id]

  os_disk {
    caching              = "ReadWrite"
    storage_account_type = "Premium_LRS"
    # Disk implicitly placed in Zone 1 to match VM
  }

  source_image_reference {
    publisher = "Canonical"
    offer     = "UbuntuServer"
    sku       = "22_04-lts"
    version   = "latest"
  }
}

# Zone-redundant SQL Database (no zone property; flag on DB resource)
resource "azurerm_mssql_database" "zone_redundant" {
  name           = "mydb"
  server_id      = azurerm_mssql_server.example.id
  sku_name       = "BC_Gen5_8"
  zone_redundant = true   # No zone property needed; platform spreads automatically
}

# Zone-spread VMSS (zonal VMs managed as a group, spread by platform)
resource "azurerm_linux_virtual_machine_scale_set" "zone_spread" {
  name                = "myVMSS"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  sku                 = "Standard_DS2_v2"
  instances           = 3
  zones               = ["1", "2", "3"]   # Three zones, one instance per zone initially

  # ... image, os_disk, network_interface blocks ...
}
Checking Zone Configuration Across a Deployment

After deploying a zone-resilient architecture, verify that every component is configured as intended. Zone-related misconfigurations are silent at deployment time but catastrophic during an actual zone failure.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Audit zone configuration for VMs and key managed services
RG="${1:-myRG}"

echo "=== VMs and their zones ==="
az vm list -g "$RG" \
  --query "[*].{name:name, zone:zones[0]}" \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== Managed Disks and their zones ==="
az disk list -g "$RG" \
  --query "[*].{name:name, zone:zones[0], sku:sku.name}" \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== VMSS and their zones ==="
az vmss list -g "$RG" \
  --query "[*].{name:name, zones:zones}" \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== Storage accounts (zone-redundant = ZRS or GZRS) ==="
az storage account list -g "$RG" \
  --query "[*].{name:name, sku:sku.name}" \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== SQL Databases (zoneRedundant flag) ==="
az sql server list -g "$RG" --query "[*].name" -o tsv | while read SRV; do
  az sql db list -g "$RG" --server "$SRV" \
    --query "[*].{db:name, zr:zoneRedundant}" --output table
done

Zonal and zone-redundant services are complementary tools. Zonal gives you explicit control over placement for workloads that need it — primarily IaaS compute. Zone-redundant removes the placement burden for managed services and delivers near-zero RPO with no operator action during a zone failure.
Use zonal (--zone flag) for VMs, VMSS, and other IaaS resources that need explicit zone awareness or zone affinity with attached disks.
Use zone-redundant SKUs (ZRS, zone_redundant=true, Premium tier) for storage, databases, messaging, and load balancers wherever the service supports it.
Never mix a zone-redundant managed service with a single-zone compute tier and call the architecture zone-resilient — the compute layer is still the single point of failure.
Validate zone configuration with az CLI audits after every deployment; flags like --zone-redundant are silent if accidentally omitted.
Document zone model decisions in architecture diagrams so future operators understand which layer is platform-managed and which requires operator action during a zone event.
